summary: DHH shares discussion threads from Joel on Software about the wide variance in programmer productivity. The post highlights that massive productivity differences aren't unique to programming but exist across professions like surgery, writing, and sports. It also notes the disconnect between productivity and compensation, where a 10x programmer typically earns only 10% more than average.
key_points: Extreme productivity variance (28x) exists across many professions, not just programming
Compensation fails to reflect productivity differences — a 10x programmer earns roughly 10% more
The market underprices top talent relative to their output
money_quotes: There are surgeons who are 28 times better than other surgeons. There are writers 28 times better than other writers.
A programmer who is 10 times more productive than average, typically only makes 10% more money than an average programmer.
key_insight: The enormous gap between programmer productivity and programmer compensation represents a market inefficiency that benefits employers who can identify top talent.
